data.title: Toast Is Revolutionizing The Restaurant Industry
data.description:
Toast (NYSE:TOST) has become a household name in the restaurant industry. Previously, simple POS systems were common for restaurants and required a lot of manual work, but now there is Toast that automates a lot. Toast offers smart solutions that allow restaurants to significantly increase their profit. The company has large customers such as Applebee's, which has led to a nice share price increase.

I am convinced that Toast is currently at the beginning of a strong growth phase. Restaurants are ready for digitalization and automation and in my opinion, this makes the stock an interesting choice for investors. I am not the only one who shares this opinion; many Seeking Alpha authors give Toast a "buy" or "strong buy" rating. And that strong rating also reflects the stock price because since its IPO in 2022, the stock has already risen 200%, while the S&P500 "only" returned 60%. With a P/S ratio of around 5x, the stock is dirt cheap, but the gross margins are not as high as you would expect from a tech company. Margins could improve significantly in the coming years, making the stock an interesting buy.

Toast's Is Transforming the Restaurant Industry

Toast is turning the hospitality industry upside down by digitizing it and applying AI to its platform. The hospitality industry is flourishing, and Toast indicated during the presentation at the 2024 investor day that the revenue of American restaurants has grown by a factor of 5x over the past 30 years. However, restaurants are confronted with rising costs and a tight labor market. This makes technologies that make work more efficient and very interesting. Toast offers a solution thanks to various services such as convenient ordering systems, kitchen screens, payment processing and data analysis. I therefore see a lot of potential in Toast's innovative AI solutions, such as "Sous Chef", which can help restaurants structurally increase their revenue per guest (for example, by making suggestions for menu items or targeted promotions). This gives Toast a clear advantage over competitors that do not offer these functionalities. CEO Aman Narang made it clear that these innovations are the core for future growth.

Toast doesn’t have any direct competitors that offer the full package, but there are companies that operate in the same niche, such as DoorDash, Olo, and Square. DoorDash focuses primarily on delivery services but doesn’t offer many solutions specifically aimed at restaurant management. Olo specializes in digital ordering but lacks hardware and insight into restaurant operational processes. Square offers a wide range of solutions but lacks, for example, extensive table management features, multi-location software, and the like. Toast now operates in 13% of the total U.S. restaurant market and has plenty of growth opportunities, both in terms of number of restaurants (globally) and revenue per restaurant.

Personally, I believe Toast’s fully integrated solutions suite is a strong competitive advantage that differentiates the company from its competitors. And recent partnerships with major chains like Applebee’s reinforce my belief that Toast is well positioned to achieve further growth by attracting more large customers.

Rising Gross Profit Signals Long-Term Growth

The Q1 2025 results show strong growth, with revenue up 24% YoY to $1.34 billion, which were driven by strong results from payment processing and software subscriptions. While hardware is not yet profitable, this segment is essential for maintaining long-term customer relationships.

I am currently less satisfied about the gross profit margin because I expect gross profit margins of 60% or higher from a software company. Toast's gross margin is just 39%, which is due to its loss-making hardware segment. Gross margin will increase as Toast continues to grow and receives more revenue from subscription services.

In the past quarter, we already saw a large improvement in gross profit to $346 million, thanks in part to economies of scale. And operating profit was $43 million compared to a loss in the same period last year. In my opinion, these results indicate strong improvements that we expect to continue in the coming years.

What I also like is Toast’s strong balance sheet. With $1.5 billion in cash and virtually no debt, it is less vulnerable to interest rate hikes and doesn’t need to raise cash through equity issuances for future investments.

Risks To Mention

There are some risks to watch out for. Competitors like DoorDash and Square are constantly introducing new services and solutions that put them in direct competition with Toast, which could put pressure on margins. However, I expect Toast, with its specialization (and kitchen screens, etc.), to mitigate this risk.

From a business perspective, the restaurant industry is also very cyclical. A recession could cause restaurants to be less likely to invest in the business optimizations and improvements that Toast offers, which could limit Toast’s growth. However, since Toast already has a strong balance sheet with ample reserves and is profitable, I expect the company to be well-prepared to weather such periods.

A potential increase in import tariffs is also a risk for Toast, which could also put pressure on margins. However, I am confident that Toast can absorb these additional costs thanks to its strong competitive position and potentially pass them on to customers without endangering the competition.

In summary, I think these risks outweigh Toast’s enormous growth potential. Investors looking for a growth stock with good prospects in a profitable company see Toast as an excellent investment.

Valuation

Growth stocks invest their cash flow back into the business, which is why Toast currently does not pay a dividend. This is a smart strategy as Toast is better positioned to invest its resources into further expansion.

In terms of valuation, Toast is trading at a P/S ratio of around 5.2x, which compares favorably to DoorDash (8x) while being comparable to smaller players like Olo (5x). I think the current valuation is attractive and offers room for further price appreciation, especially if Toast can continue to improve its profit margins.

Conclusion

Toast is well positioned to benefit from digitalization and AI in the hospitality industry. Profit margins are growing steadily and Toast is showing structural improvements, while the stock valuation remains very attractive compared to competitors.

Therefore, I give Toast a "strong buy" rating. The strong balance sheet, growing customer base and strong dominance in the restaurant niche market give me confidence that Toast will continue to perform strongly in the coming years. I also expect that the continued investments in innovative technologies such as AI will help the company maintain and even expand its market share.

data.title: IHH Healthcare: Focus On Near-Term Headwinds And Mid-Term Re-Rating Potential (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
I've become Neutral on IHH Healthcare Berhad (OTCPK:IHHHF) (IHHH.KL) or "IHB". Its 1H24 financial metrics and new hospital bed pipeline were detailed in the earlier September 1, 2024 update.

IHB's rating is revised from Buy to Hold. My downgrade considers weaker Q1 results and multiple expansion triggers for the longer run.

I'm Expecting Growth Deceleration In The Latest Quarter

The group is releasing its 1Q25 financials next week.

My projections point to IHHHF's topline and EBIT rising by 12 percent and 7% YoY to RM$6.7 billion and MYR 1,037M, respectively for the first three months of 2025.

It had performed relatively better in 4Q2024 with a 26% revenue increase and a 34% operating income rise.

IHH's FY24 Sales Split By Segment

In 1Q25, the company's operations in certain geographical markets should have been under pressure.

IHB's Malaysian ("MY") unit is likely to be impacted by wider price discounts.

The MY government has required insurance businesses to limit healthcare premium hikes by no more than a tenth this year. This in turn led to insurers demanding that local hospitals keep medical expenses unchanged during the same period.

Its Singapore ("SG") division has to temporarily run on a lower capacity.

MEO ("Mount Elizabeth Orchard"), a key SG property for IHHHF, is currently in the process of refurbishment which is scheduled to complete by September '25. In the interim, this hospital will operate with roughly half the beds that it used to have.

For the recent financial period, the Turkish ("TR") segment could have faced headwinds pertaining to FX ("Foreign Exchange") translation losses.

The USD/TRY exchange rate went from 35 to 38 in the first quarter of the year. The depreciation of TR's domestic currency was driven by negative sentiment surrounding the apprehending of a political figure.

IHHHF's sole bright spot is India ("IN") where the group intends to have an incremental 470 beds in full-year 2025. This is 18% more than the Indian arm's actual additions last year.

It's hard for me to remain bullish on IHB when I anticipate that its 1Q numbers will be disappointing.

But IHB's Peer Discount Might Narrow If Medium-Term Catalysts Materialize

I'm not ready to rate IHHHF as a Sell, despite the negative takeaways from my first quarter results preview.

Asian Healthcare Listed Company Comparison

Stock Consensus NTM ("Next Twelve Months") EV/EBITDA Consensus Current Fiscal Year ROA ("Return On Assets") Forecast
IHH 13 3.6%
KPJ (OTC:KPJHF) (KPJH.HL) 16 5.3%
Apollo Hospitals (APLH.NS) 29 10.1%

IHB is trading at a lower EBITDA multiple as compared to its peers. The group's current undervaluation is justified by its inferior financial returns, but that could change going forward.

In my opinion, one way to enhance ROA will be shrinking the asset base ("denominator of ROA equation") with surplus cash distributions.

IHHHF generated OCF ("Operating Cash Flow") of MYR 4.3B in FY24. But its dividends cash outflow was a fraction of that at RM$900 million in the same period.

The company's shareholder return policy stipulates a minimum DPR ("Dividend Payout Ratio") of 30 percent. This is far lower than the 48% worldwide mean distribution payout. Also, Malaysian peer KPJ allocated a more generous 54% of its 2024 bottom-line to cash distributions.

At its 4Q2024 analyst briefing ("source: S&P Capital IQ transcript") in late-Feb, IHB stressed that it is focused on "delivering value to shareholders". You wouldn't rule out the possibility of the group benchmarking its future dividends with global and domestic comparables.

Separately, there are opportunities for the company to restructure its business portfolio to achieve higher net profit ("numerator of ROA equation").

It can possibly put up some of its non-core ("non-hospital") businesses for sale. For example, IHB's laboratories segment reported a 21 percent EBITDA margin last year. During this time frame, the EBITDA-to-sales ratios for IHHHF's group-level and SG business were superior at 23% and 30%, respectively.

Divesting lower-margin operations is something that could boost IHH's overall profitability.

Also, IHB can realize financial cost savings through deleveraging.

Its net debt-to-EBITDA metric rose from 1.6 times at end-FY23 to 2.4x as of December 31, 2024. This led to interest expenses jumping by +13% in the prior year.

IHHHF highlighted at the group's February results call that it is a "cash-generative business" which will "tend to pay down our debt" when "we have cash". It's reasonable to think that its gearing will eventually trend downwards, which is positive for future earnings.

My Final Thoughts

A Hold for IHH is fair.

I'm no longer a bull on the name after previewing its 1Q25 performance.

But a bearish view is unjustified, as the stock has re-rating potential in the intermediate-to-long term.

IHHHF's current valuations are reasonable. The low-single digit ROA and unfavorable Q1 expectations mean that it is deserving of a peer discount for now.

data.title: BRF Q1: This Merger Changes The Whole Thesis (Downgrade)
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I am downgrading my recommendation from buy to hold on BRF (BRFS) shares following the release of its Q1 results and the announcement of a proposed merger with Marfrig (OTCPK:MRRTY).

The results were strong, featuring increased revenues, margin expansion, and healthy leverage, however, a proposed merger could completely change the thesis, as we will see below.

Corporate Profile

BRF is a Brazilian multinational meatpacking company with over 90 years of operations. The company has over 100,000 employees and operates in 120 countries. BRF produces 5 million tons of food per year.

One of the differences is the business model that has broad global diversification with 101 distribution centers and 45 industrial facilities in South America, Africa, Europe, Asia, and the Middle East.

Revisiting The Thesis

My first article about BRF was published on July 5, 2024. At the time, the recommendation was to buy the shares. The company had a prominent position in its market, high margins, and extremely low multiples, however, we have more data to analyze.

Next, I will provide a full description of the company's results and link this to the merger proposal and developments. It is important to note that the company is Brazilian and publishes its results in the local currency, so I will convert to dollars using the exchange rate 1 USD = 5.72 BRL, as this was the exchange rate on the last day of the quarter.

Net Revenue

The company's net revenue varied 16% y/y and -11.6% to BRL 15.5 billion ($2.7 billion). The reasons for the annual increase were the 7.7% growth in average volume and 7.6% in average price, while the decrease in the quarterly comparison is explained by seasonality and hyperinflation in Turkey.

The outlook is dependent on the success of the merger with Marfrig. If the transaction is successfully completed, the resulting company should have revenues of BRL 220 billion ($38 billion per year).

Costs, Expenses, and Margins

COGS varied 12.9% y/y and -12.4% q/q to BRL 11.5 billion ($2 billion). Inflation in goods and services, together with rising costs on the Turkish platform, were the reasons behind the variation.

Given the excellent control of costs and expenses, we had a 190 bps increase in the adjusted EBITDA margin to 17.7%. The company estimates that the merger could bring a gain of BRL 805 million ($140 million) per year in EBITDA, which is currently BRL 2.75 billion ($481 million).

It is worth remembering that Marfrig could use BRF's entire sales structure in the Middle East and nearby locations to sell Marfrig's beef.

Leverage

The company ended the first quarter with a healthy leverage of 0.54x net debt/adjusted EBITDA, the lowest in history. Here comes one of the main points that makes me skeptical about the thesis for now.

Marfrig is currently leveraged at over 3x net debt/adjusted EBITDA, and this would impact the new company's results initially. This leverage should also imply a better exchange ratio for shares, but I will discuss this in more detail.

Net Income

Net income doubled year over year and rose 36.6% q/q to BRL 1.18 billion ($207 million). Net margin rose 3.2% y/y to 7.6%, as we can see below.

In my view, the quarterly results were solid. The company reported an increase in revenues, an expansion of margins, and a business model that is proving to be increasingly resilient. The big point of doubt is the merger proposed by Marfrig. I brought some insights into each segment above, but now we are able to talk solely about this business.

Merger and Valuation

In the potential transaction, BRF shares will be incorporated into Marfrig at an exchange ratio of 0.8521 Marfrig shares for each BRF share. The transaction also provides for a dividend payment of BRL 2.5 billion ($437 million) from Marfrig and BRL 3.2 billion from BRF ($559 million).

In my view, the exchange ratio is much more favorable for Marfrig, so much so that BRF shares fell 12% in the aftermarket after the news was released. The fact is that Marfrig is much more leveraged, and its market cap is half that of BRF. Although BRF's valuation is cheap, as we can see below, there are several factors that make the risk/return ratio less attractive in my view.

There is no doubt that the new company will have synergies, in addition to several tax gains. However, after the distribution of dividends, I estimate that the new company will have a leverage of 2.9x EBITDA, a relatively high leverage in my view.

Additionally, there is a long way to go to consolidate all assets, and this type of operation does not appeal to me as an analyst, so I will downgrade the recommendation from buy to hold and wait for the operation's developments.

Potential Threats To The Thesis

There are several risks to this thesis. The merger will likely involve redomiciling the new company and changing its listing. This could increase the liquidity of its shares.

A possible listing in the US would provide access to a much broader capital market with higher multiples, and the company could also manage its debt with more affordable interest rates. All of these arguments could make the stock soar.

Last but not least, Seeking Alpha's powerful Quant tools recommend a strong buy of the shares. This contrasts with my more skeptical view.

The Bottom Line

BRF reported excellent results in the first quarter. The thesis of my first article has materialized from an operational point of view, as the company is even more robust and has higher margins.

However, the possible merger will completely change the thesis. It is true that the new company will be even more robust, but until that happens, we will have a leveraged company that is in the process of cutting costs and expenses.

Based on this analysis, I am downgrading my recommendation on BRF shares from buy to hold. Although BRF's valuation remains attractive, I have doubts about the new company and prefer to wait to have more details.

data.title: Rates Spark: Steeper Curves Amid U.S. Concerns
data.description:
By Benjamin Schroeder

Weak 20Y Treasury sale spurs further long end sell-off

Long-end rates remain under pressure. And the divergent narratives on the two sides of the Atlantic were underscored by the market’s reception of the scheduled government bond supply.

While the upward pressure is a more global phenomenon which also lifted EUR interest rates as the path for the Dutch pension reform was cleared, yields here initially managed to retreat from their peaks after strong demand in the 10y Bund auction.

But that recovery did not carry over into the later US session. In a market increasingly attuned to concerns about deteriorating US government creditworthiness, a weaker 20y US Treasury auction led to a spike in yields. While the 20y maturity has a tendency to be a trickier sell, the results were enough to push 30y yields beyond 5%, ending the day up by 10bp. The 10y yield rose to 4.58%. Risk assets were not able to detach themselves, with equities selling off alongside.

Amid the worries around rising US deficits, a key focus of today will be the vote on the US tax bill that will likely take place early on Thursday after the bill passed the House Rules Committee. But also on the European side, any improvement in the PMI and Ifo releases today could reinforce the bearish steepening tendencies, with slight improvements expected on the back of the recent de-escalation on the tariff front. We doubt that markets will place too much weight on the flash PMIs, as the trade backdrop remains volatile, especially with a view to the EU.

Today’s events and market view

The highlight of the day will be the eurozone flash PMIs. The consensus is looking for a slight improvement with the composite index seen nudging up to 50.6 from 50.4. Germany will release its Ifo at the same time.

There will also be more comments from European Central Bank officials to digest, including hawkish voices like Robert Holzmann and Joachim Nagel, but also Boris Vujcic, Luis de Guindos and Jose Luis Escriva. The ECB will also release the minutes for the April meeting.

On the US side, the S&P PMIs are not expected to move much but usually get less attention than the ISMs. The weekly jobless claims numbers are also seen as stable. Federal Reserve speakers are Tom Barkin and John Williams.

The supply side will be busier with Spanish and French auctions. Spain will sell up to €6.5bn across 3y to 17y bonds and France will sell up to €12bn in 3y to 7y bonds and an additional €2bn in inflation linked bonds. The US Treasury will sell US$18bn in 10y TIPS.
